sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A lab studied selective attention using a color-naming Stroop task. Participants named the ink color of printed words under either quiet conditions or with background cafeteria noise. Reaction times were recorded for congruent trials (the word meaning matched the ink color) and incongruent trials (the word meaning conflicted with the ink color). In quiet conditions, mean reaction times were 540 ms for congruent trials and 690 ms for incongruent trials. With noise, the means were 580 ms and 760 ms. Error rates also rose slightly under noise, especially on incongruent trials.
questions.stem: Which condition had the slowest mean reaction time?

explanations.B: Correct. The tallest bar is the noise-incongruent condition at 760 ms. It exceeds quiet-incongruent at 690 ms, noise-congruent at 580 ms, and quiet-congruent at 540 ms.
explanations.A: Quiet incongruent was slower than both congruent conditions but still faster than noise incongruent.
explanations.C: Noise congruent is much faster than both incongruent conditions.
explanations.D: Quiet congruent is the fastest condition in the figure.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean reaction time for congruent and incongruent Stroop trials under quiet and noisy conditions.

questions.stem: Why are incongruent trials slower than congruent trials?

choices.A: Participants must inhibit the automatic tendency to read the word
choices.B: The retina adapts more slowly to colored ink
choices.C: Congruent words are physically larger on the screen

explanations.A: Correct. The Stroop effect arises because reading is highly practiced and automatic. On incongruent trials, participants must suppress the word meaning and instead respond to the ink color, which creates conflict and slows responding.
explanations.B: Retinal adaptation does not explain the semantic conflict in Stroop performance.
explanations.C: The passage gives no indication that stimulus size differed by condition.
explanations.D: The task is the same basic task in both conditions; motivation is not removed.

questions.stem: What does the effect of background noise most likely indicate?

choices.C: Noise further taxes selective attention and interference control
choices.A: Noise improves executive control by increasing arousal without limit
choices.B: Noise eliminates the Stroop effect entirely
choices.D: Noise only affects congruent trials and not incongruent trials

explanations.C: Correct. Reaction times rose in both congruent and incongruent conditions under noise, and the rise was especially large for incongruent trials. That pattern suggests that distraction burdens attention and makes conflict resolution harder.
explanations.A: The data show worse, not better, control under noise.
explanations.B: The Stroop effect remains present because incongruent trials are still slower than congruent trials.
explanations.D: Incongruent trials are clearly affected by noise, and in fact they show a larger cost.

questions.stem: A student who studies in a crowded cafeteria may experience a problem most analogous to which pattern in the passage?

choices.D: More distraction and slower performance on tasks requiring concentration
choices.A: Improved color vision from background sound
choices.B: Complete inability to process any information
choices.C: A change limited only to sensory adaptation

explanations.D: Correct. The noisy Stroop condition illustrates how distracting environments can slow performance when attention and interference control are needed. Studying complex material in a crowded setting would plausibly produce a similar concentration cost.
explanations.A: Background noise does not improve color vision in the study.
explanations.B: Participants still performed the task; the issue is reduced efficiency, not total inability.
explanations.C: The pattern is cognitive and attentional, not merely sensory adaptation.

questions.stem: Which statement is most justified by the data?

choices.C: Noise and incongruence both contribute to slower responding, and their combination produces the worst performance
choices.A: Noise mattered only when the trial was congruent
choices.B: Incongruence mattered only in quiet conditions
choices.D: The study proves that cafeteria noise causes long-term memory loss

explanations.C: Correct. The figure shows separate costs for noise and for incongruence, with the highest reaction time when both are present together. The study therefore supports an additive burden on performance in this selective-attention task.
explanations.A: Noise affected both congruent and incongruent trials, not only congruent trials.
explanations.B: Incongruence mattered in both sound conditions.
explanations.D: The task measures immediate attentional performance, not long-term memory loss.

questions.passage: A perception study used a simulated airport-screening task in which participants decided whether a faint target item was present in an X-ray image. One group completed the task after a normal night's sleep, and another completed it after restricted sleep. Researchers calculated hit rate, false-alarm rate, and d' (a signal-detection sensitivity index). The well-rested group had a hit rate of 0.81, a false-alarm rate of 0.18, and d' = 1.90. The sleep-restricted group had a hit rate of 0.69, a false-alarm rate of 0.27, and d' = 1.20. The sleep-restricted participants also reported lower alertness.
questions.stem: Which group showed better sensitivity on the screening task?

choices.D: Sensitivity cannot be inferred from the figure

explanations.B: Correct. The well-rested group has a higher d' value, 1.90 versus 1.20. Because d' indexes sensitivity, the well-rested participants were better at distinguishing target-present from target-absent images.
explanations.A: The sleep-restricted group had lower d', indicating worse sensitivity.
explanations.C: The d' values are clearly different.
explanations.D: Sensitivity is explicitly represented by d' in the figure.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Hit rate, false-alarm rate, and sensitivity (d") for well-rested versus sleep-restricted participants.

questions.stem: What does the higher false-alarm rate in the sleep-restricted group mean?

choices.A: They more often said the target was present when it was actually absent
choices.B: They missed fewer true targets than the well-rested group
choices.C: They responded more slowly but with perfect accuracy
choices.D: They were better at separating signal from noise

explanations.A: Correct. A false alarm occurs when a participant incorrectly reports that the signal is present. The sleep-restricted group’s higher false-alarm rate therefore reflects more frequent incorrect “present” responses on target-absent trials.
explanations.B: Their hit rate was actually lower, not better.
explanations.C: The passage discusses hit rate, false alarms, and d', not perfect accuracy.
explanations.D: The lower d' indicates poorer, not better, discrimination between signal and noise.

questions.stem: The lower d' in sleep-restricted participants most directly suggests impairment in which function?

explanations.D: Correct. Signal-detection sensitivity depends on accurately extracting weak information from noisy input. The lower d' after restricted sleep suggests worse vigilance and poorer perceptual discrimination.
explanations.A: Assimilation is a Piagetian concept unrelated to sleep-related perceptual sensitivity.
explanations.B: Primary socialization concerns learning norms and values in childhood.
explanations.C: Role strain concerns competing demands within a role.

questions.stem: If an employer wanted to reduce missed or mistaken detections, what policy would be most consistent with the passage?

choices.B: Avoid assigning high-stakes screening tasks to sleep-restricted workers
choices.A: Increase work shifts after sleep loss to build tolerance
choices.C: Rely only on workers’ subjective confidence rather than performance tests
choices.D: Add more visual clutter to the screening images

explanations.B: Correct. The sleep-restricted group showed both fewer hits and more false alarms, indicating worse screening performance. The most evidence-consistent policy is therefore to protect critical detection tasks from fatigue-related impairment.
explanations.A: The study offers no evidence that additional fatigue improves accuracy.
explanations.C: Subjective confidence is not shown to substitute for objective performance.
explanations.D: More clutter would likely worsen, not improve, detection.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the data?

choices.A: Restricted sleep was associated with both lower hit rates and more false alarms in this task
choices.B: Sleep restriction improved response criterion without changing sensitivity
choices.C: False alarms decreased after restricted sleep
choices.D: The study proves every sleep-restricted worker will fail the task

explanations.A: Correct. The figure directly shows a drop in hit rate from 0.81 to 0.69 and an increase in false alarms from 0.18 to 0.27. That pattern supports poorer task performance under sleep restriction without justifying extreme claims about every individual worker.
explanations.B: Sensitivity changed as well, so the claim that only criterion shifted is not supported.
explanations.C: False alarms moved upward, not downward.
explanations.D: The data describe group averages, not certainty about every individual.
